facts.title: A Nobel for Korean literature
facts.fact: In 2024, novelist Han Kang became the first Korean to win the Nobel Prize in Literature, recognized for prose that confronts historical trauma.
facts.korean_en: The author Han Kang won the Nobel Prize in Literature.
facts.note: A nice coincidence: the author **한강** is written exactly like the **한강** (Han River).

facts.title: The sijo, a three-line poem
facts.fact: The sijo is a traditional three-line Korean poem dating back to the Goryeo dynasty — short and structured, comparable in spirit to the Japanese haiku.
facts.korean_en: Sijo is a traditional Korean poem.
facts.note: A **시조** has three lines of about 14–16 syllables each.

facts.title: Hong Gildong, a heroic outlaw
facts.fact: **The Tale of Hong Gildong** is often called Korea's first novel written in hangul. Its hero is the gifted son of a nobleman and a concubine who, blocked from advancement, becomes a Robin Hood-like leader of outlaws.
facts.korean_en: The Tale of Hong Gildong is an old novel written in hangul.
facts.note: The name **홍길동** is still used today the way English uses "John Doe" on sample forms.

facts.title: Chunhyang, a famous love story
facts.fact: **The Tale of Chunhyang** is a beloved classic about a commoner's daughter who stays faithful to her noble husband despite a cruel governor's threats. It began as a pansori song before becoming a written tale.
facts.korean_en: The Tale of Chunhyang is a very famous love story.
facts.note: The city of **남원** holds a yearly festival in honor of **춘향**.

facts.title: Kim Sowol and 'Azaleas'
facts.fact: Kim Sowol is one of Korea's most loved modern poets. His poem **진달래꽃** ("Azaleas") speaks of letting go of a departing love with quiet grace, and many Koreans know it by heart.
facts.korean_en: Kim Sowol wrote many beautiful poems.
facts.note: **진달래** (azalea) blooms in spring and is a common image in Korean poetry.

facts.title: Yun Dong-ju, a poet of conscience
facts.fact: Yun Dong-ju wrote gentle, searching poems during Japanese colonial rule and died in prison in 1945, just before liberation. His posthumous collection made him a national symbol of quiet resistance.
facts.korean_en: Yun Dong-ju wrote poems during the Japanese colonial period.
facts.note: His poems often reflect on the **하늘** (sky) and personal honesty.

facts.title: The Vegetarian wins the Booker
facts.fact: Han Kang's novel **The Vegetarian** won the International Booker Prize in 2016, shared with its translator Deborah Smith. The win brought a wave of global attention to contemporary Korean fiction.
facts.korean_en: This novel won a prize in 2016.
facts.note: The Korean title is **채식주의자**, literally "vegetarian person."

facts.title: Pansori, sung storytelling
facts.fact: Pansori is a traditional performance where a single singer narrates a long story, accompanied only by a drummer. UNESCO recognized it as Intangible Cultural Heritage in 2003.
facts.korean: 판소리는 한 사람이 부르는 긴 노래입니다.
facts.korean_en: Pansori is a long song sung by one person.
facts.note: The drummer sometimes shouts **얼쑤!** to cheer the singer on.

facts.title: Heungbu, Nolbu, and a swallow
facts.fact: In the folktale **Heungbu and Nolbu**, a kind poor brother heals a swallow's broken leg and is rewarded with riches, while his greedy rich brother's selfishness brings him ruin.
facts.note: Magic seeds grow from a **박** (gourd) — a classic image in this tale.

facts.title: Sim Cheong's devotion
facts.fact: The folktale of **Sim Cheong** tells of a devoted daughter who sacrifices herself to the sea to restore her blind father's sight. Her piety moves the heavens, and she returns as a queen.
facts.korean_en: Sim Cheong loved her father very much.
facts.note: The story is a famous example of **효** (filial piety) in Korean culture.

facts.title: A proverb about beginnings
facts.fact: Korean is rich in **속담** (proverbs). A well-known one says that even a great task is already half done once you begin, encouraging people not to fear starting.
facts.korean_en: Starting is half the battle. (lit. "The beginning is half.")
facts.note: This is one of the first **속담** many Korean learners memorize.

facts.title: A proverb about kind words
facts.fact: Another common proverb teaches that if you speak kindly first, others will repay your kindness — a reminder that polite words shape how people treat you.
facts.korean: 가는 말이 고와야 오는 말이 곱다.
facts.korean_en: Kind words going out bring kind words coming back.
facts.note: Koreans often quote this to remind someone to speak politely.

facts.title: The rise of web novels
facts.fact: **Web novels** (웹소설) are a booming part of Korean reading culture, published chapter by chapter on phone apps. Many popular ones are later adapted into webtoons or TV dramas.
facts.korean_en: These days people read web novels on their phones.
facts.note: Readers often pay a small fee to unlock each new **회** (episode/chapter).

facts.title: Children's Day and a writer
facts.fact: Bang Jeong-hwan, an early champion of children's literature, helped create Korea's Children's Day. He is also credited with popularizing the respectful word **어린이** for children.
facts.note: He chose **어린이** to treat children with the same respect as adults.

facts.title: A bestselling novel about a mother
facts.fact: Shin Kyung-sook's novel **Please Look After Mom** became an international bestseller. It follows a family searching for their elderly mother after she goes missing in a Seoul subway station.
facts.korean_en: This novel is a story about a mother.
facts.note: The Korean title **엄마를 부탁해** means "Please take care of Mom."

facts.title: Diaries and essays (수필)
facts.fact: The essay, called **수필** in Korean, is a popular literary form prized for its honest, personal voice. Keeping a diary (일기) is also a long-standing habit taught to schoolchildren.
facts.korean_en: I write in my diary every day.
facts.note: Writing a daily **일기** is a common way for learners to practice Korean.

facts.title: Hangul Day honors the alphabet
facts.fact: October 9th is **Hangul Day** (한글날), celebrating King Sejong's creation of the Korean alphabet in the 15th century. The alphabet made reading and writing accessible to ordinary people.
facts.note: King **세종대왕** is honored for making writing easy to learn.

facts.title: A modern storyteller
facts.fact: Kim Young-ha is a leading contemporary novelist whose work has been widely translated. His sharp, modern stories often explore memory, identity, and life in today's Korea.
facts.korean: 그 작가의 책은 여러 나라에서 읽힙니다.
facts.korean_en: That author's books are read in many countries.
facts.note: **현대 소설** (modern fiction) is a large category in any Korean bookstore.
